coredata.dc:title: Two-photon-excited tumor cell fluorescence targeted imaging based on transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Hong-Li
He, Xi-Wen
Li, Wen-You
Zhang, Yu-Kui
coredata.dc:description: Transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles (Trf-SiNPs) were fabricated and utilized for targeted fluorescence imaging in tumor cells. Silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) was firstly synthesized by microwave irradiation method, and then coupled with transferrin in the presence of 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). The structural informations of Trf-SiNPs were measured by transmission electron microscope and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The optical properties of Trf-SiNPs were characterized by ultraviolet absorption spectrum, fluorescence emission spectrum, fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence lifetime, photo-stability, and so on. MTT assay evidenced the low toxicity of Trf-SiNPs. Finally, Trf-SiNPs were successfully applied in HeLa cells and HepG2 cells for targeted fluorescence imaging under single-photon excitation and two-photon excitation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-phonon excitation
Transferrin
Silicon nanoparticles
Tumor cells
Targeted fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro targeted imaging of Trf-SiNPs
In vitro targeted two-photon imaging of Trf-SiNPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 22074069 and 21775077 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structural characterizations of Trf-SiNPs. (A) TEM image; (B) Size distribution histogram; (C) Circular dichroism spectra of Trf and Trf-SiNPs; (D) FTIR spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Trf, SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs; (B) fluorescence excitation spectrum (Ex) and fluorescence emission spectrum (Em) of Trf-SiNPs. Insets: photos of Trf-SiNPs aqueous in daylight and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation; (C) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of Trf-SiNPs with the excitation wavelength enlarging from 300 to 430 nm; (D) Upconversion fluorescence spectra of Trf-SiNPs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 600 to 800 nm.
Optical characterizations of Trf-SiNPs. (A) Spectra of absolute quantum yield; (B) Curves of relative quantum yields; (C) Fluorescence decay curves of SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs; (D) Photo-stability.
pH stability of Trf-SiNPs. (A) In water; (B) In PBS.
Viability of HeLa cells after being incubated for 24 and 48 h with the Trf-SiNPs concentrations of 0 ∼ 200 μg/mL.

(A) The confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with 100 μg/mL SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs for 8 h. (B) The confocal fluorescence images of HepG2 cells and HeLa cells incubated with 50 μg/mL Trf-SiNPs for 8 h.

(A) Two-photon fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with 100 μg/mL SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs for 8 h. (B) Two-photon fluorescence images of HepG2 cells and HeLa cells incubated with 100 μg/mL Trf-SiNPs for 8 h.
The schematic illustration of Trf-SiNPs for targeted imaging in tumor cells.

head.ce:title: Two-photon-excited tumor cell fluorescence targeted imaging based on transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles (Trf-SiNPs) with two-photon-excited fluorescence were fabricated and utilized for targeted fluorescence imaging in tumor cells.

Transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles (Trf-SiNPs) were fabricated and utilized for targeted fluorescence imaging in tumor cells. Silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) was firstly synthesized by microwave irradiation method, and then coupled with transferrin in the presence of 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). The structural informations of Trf-SiNPs were measured by transmission electron microscope and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The optical properties of Trf-SiNPs were characterized by ultraviolet absorption spectrum, fluorescence emission spectrum, fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence lifetime, photo-stability, and so on. MTT assay evidenced the low toxicity of Trf-SiNPs. Finally, Trf-SiNPs were successfully applied in HeLa cells and HepG2 cells for targeted fluorescence imaging under single-photon excitation and two-photon excitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Imaging technology uses probes to reveal complex biological processes (gene expression, signal transduction, interactions between proteins, etc.) into intuitive images to carry out the real-time, in vivo, dynamic, qualitative and quantitative imaging [1–3]. According to different principles, imaging technologies can be divided into fluorescence imaging (FI), X-ray computer-aided tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), single-photon emission tomography (SPECT), Raman imaging (RI), positron emission scanning (PET), ultrasound imaging, mass spectrometry imaging, etc. [4,5]. Among them, fluorescence imaging has the advantages of higher sensitivity, rapidity, lossless images and so on [6–9]. Combing with the targeted technology, fluorescence imaging has been significantly improved in selectivity for the specific binding between the targeted molecular and its receptor [10,11]. Cancer is one of the crucial deadliest malignancy, whose surface can overexpress the targeted molecular receptor [12,13]. Transferrin (Trf) is one of glycoprotein and plays a vital role in biological function in transportation of iron. Trf allows highly sufficient site-specific targeting to transferring receptor (TrfR), which express on tumor cells up to 100-fold higher than the average expression of normal cells [14]. Thereby, transferrin-functionalized fluorescence nanomaterials can be explored to fabricate the targeted imaging probes to specifically recognize tumor cells [15–17].

Organic fluorescence dyes [18], conventional quantum dots (e.g.Ⅱ-Ⅵ elements) [19], gold or silver nanoclusters [20], etc. were usually adopted as the fluorescence probes for bioimaging. However, organic fluorescent dyes generally have poor photobleaching thresholds for their intrinsic photophysical properties. Quantum dots contain heavy metals to cause the potential cytotoxicity in biology. Gold/silver nanoclusters possess the high synthetic cost. These disadvantages restricted their effectiveness in bioimaging. Therefore, it is of great significance to design a novel fluorescence nanomaterial featuring advantages of low cost, good photo-stability and environmentally friendly for bioimaging.

Silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) have been received widespread attention as a novel biological fluorescence probe due to their outstanding metrics of rich in source, simple preparation, controllable surface, excellent optical properties, strong resistance to photobleaching and low biological toxicity [21–24]. Furthermore, SiNPs exhibited the excellent upconversion property, which might be attributed to the two-photon absorption process [25,26]. The fluorescence imaging based on two-photon excitation theory could reduce the interference of auto-fluorescence from biological tissues for the low photoluminescence energy and endow strong tissue permeability into living biology [27,28]. Some researchers have utilized SiNPs for two-photon excitation fluorescence imaging [29–31] and manufactured the transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles (Trf-SiNPs) for in vivo imaging [32–34]. However, the reported silicon nanoparticles were operational complexity and prepared by using the hazardous reagents (e.g., HF, toluene), which was not environmentally friendly [35–37]. In addition, two-photon-excitation fluorescence imaging of transferrin-functionalized silicon nanoparticles has not been yet reported.

Herein, we presented a facile targeted two-photon-excitation fluorescence imaging probe of Trf-SiNPs. SiNPs was firstly prepared by microwave irradiation method, then coupled with transferrin by the interaction between carboxylic and amino groups in the presence of EDC and NHS. The as-synthesized Trf-SiNPs emitted blue fluorescence with the quantum yield of 28.8 %, strong stability, long lifetime (14.59 ns), excellent up-conversion property and low cytotoxicity. Finally, Trf-SiNPs was successfully applied in targeted tumor cell imaging under single-photon excitation and two-photon excitation.

Holo-transferrin was purchased from Sigma-Aldtich Scientific Ltd (Shanghai, China). N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenesiamine (DAMO, ≥ 99.5%), glycerol, disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), trisodium citrate dehydrate (≥99.0%), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Human cervical carcinoma (HeLa) cell lines, and human liver carcinoma (HepG2) cell lines were provided by Nankai University School of Medicine. 3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT, Keygen Biotech, Jiangsu, China) was used to determine the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared Trf-SiNPs. All chemicals were employed without additional purification. Ultrapure water (Aquapro, 18.2 MΩ·cm) was utilized throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was captured by a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (FEI, The Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum (4000–400 cm−1) was measured by a Vector 22 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on a J-715 spectropolarimeter (JASCO, Japan). A UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) were employed to record the UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and the fluorescence spectra, respectively. All the fluorescence intensity was measured with the excitation and emission slit width of 5 nm, and the voltage of 700 V. The relative quantum yield (RQY) was calculated using the freshly prepared quinine sulphate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4. The absolute quantum yield (AQY) and the fluorescence decay curve were measured by the FLS-920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK) equipped with an integration sphere attachment. The fluorescence imaging photographs excited at 405 nm were captured by FV 1000 confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) with the emission window of 500–600 nm. The two-photon fluorescence images of Trf-SiNPs excited at 760 nm were measured by a Leica TSC SP8 two-photon laser confocal fluorescence microscopy (Leica, Germany) with the emission window of 500–600 nm.

SiNPs were prepared according to the previous reported [38]. Briefly, 0.3180 g of citrate and 8 mL glycerol were added into Ar-saturated round bottom and then dramatically stirred to mix well for 20 min. The homogeneous system was stirred for another 10 min after the addition of 3 mL DAMO. The precursor solution was transferred into the microwave reactor and reacted at 180 ° C for 15 min under normal pressure to yield SiNPs, which were purified by dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da) and freeze-dried for further characterizations and applications.

The conjugation of transferrin with SiNPs was performed as follows. Typically, 50 μL of the holo-Trf solution (10 mg/mL) was activated with 50 μL EDC (10 mg/mL) and 125 μL NHS (10 mg/mL) at 37 ° C.The stock solution gently stirred for 30 min in dark. Then, 1 mL SiNPs solution (100 μg/mL) was added into the reaction system, which sequentially stirred in dark at 37 ° C overnight. Trf-SiNPs were purified by dialysis (MWCO: 80 KDa), freeze-dried and stored at 4 ° C in dark for further applications.

HCl and NaOH were adopted for adjusting the wide pH in water from 2 to 12. Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O were utilized to regulate the phosphate buffer solutions with the various pH from 4.5 to 9.5. The stock solutions were utilized to estimate the pH-stability of Trf-SiNPs.

The cytotoxicity of Trf-SiNPs in HeLa cells was evaluated by MTT assay. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 4 × 103 cells per well and treated with Trf-SiNPs (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL) for 24, and 48 h, respectively. Three parallel replicates were performed for each sample. The absorbance (OD value) at the wavelength of 550 nm was measured for calculating the cell survival rate.

The HeLa cells and the HepG2 cells were cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h, and then exposed to SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs at 50, 100 μg/ml for 8 h. The control group cells were performed by the same operation with free fluorescence probe. 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.2) was used to wash the cells, which were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde in the dark and sealed on the glass slides with mounting medium. The targeted fluorescence imaging was accomplished with single-photon excitation of 405 nm and two-photon excitation of 760 nm, respectively.

The schematic illustration of Trf-SiNPs for targeted imaging in tumor cells was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, SiNPs were prepared via one-pot microwave method, which significantly increased the reaction efficiency, improved the products selectivity, and enabled the uniform particles [39]. Fig. 1 displays the structural characterizations of Trf-SiNPs. TEM image showed that the resulted Trf-SiNPs appeared good monodispersity with sphericity (Fig. 1A). As shown in Fig. 1B, the size distribution histogram of Trf-SiNPs was drawn by counting more than 240 particles and the average size was calculated to be 2.00 ± 0.03 nm. The CD spectrum of Trf-SiNPs was almost identical with that of Trf (Fig. 1C), which indicated that the spiral structure of transferrin protein barely changed during the formation of Trf-SiNPs. FTIR spectrum evidenced the surface structure of the synthetic Trf-SiNPs. As observed in Fig. 1D, the absorption peaks of 1060 cm−1, 2957 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of Si-O bond, the anti-symmetric stretching vibration of C-H bond, and the bending vibration of N-H bond, respectively. In addition, Trf-SiNPs showed the characteristic stretching and bending vibration of proteins linking amino acids such as amide I (1653 cm−1), amide II (1540 cm−1), amide III (1260 cm−1), and primary amide (3371 cm−1), which indicated the formation of the Trf-SiNPs [20].

Fig. 2 exhibits the optical properties of Trf-SiNPs. As described in Fig. 2A, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Trf and SiNPs showed an obvious absorption at 280 nm and 360 nm, respectively. After functionalization, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Trf-SiNPs was observed not only the absorption peak of 280 nm, but also the peak of 360 nm, which further proved the successful fabrication of Trf-SiNPs. Fig. 2B shows the fluorescence excitation spectrum (Ex) and fluorescence emission spectrum (Em) of Trf-SiNPs. As displayed in Fig. 2B, high symmetry fluorescence excitation peak and fluorescence emission peak of Trf-SiNPs were located at 385 nm and 480 nm, respectively. The inset images indicated that Trf-SiNPs aqueous solution were colorless under daylight and emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. With the excitation wavelength enlarging from 300 to 430 nm, the emission wavelength of Trf-SiNPs varied from 477 to 500 nm (Fig. 2C), which indicated the wavelength-dependent emission behavior of the as-prepared Trf-SiNPs. The result was in agreement with the reported literatures [40–42]. As displayed in Fig. 2D, the emission wavelength of Trf-SiNPs located at the range of 480 to 488 nm with the excitation wavelength increasing from 600 to 880 nm, which demonstrated the up-conversion property of Trf-SiNPs, generating from the two-photon absorption [26,29,43]. Thereby, the as-prepared Trf-SiNPs should have a good ability of two-photon excitation imaging.

AQY of Trf-SiNPs was measured to be 28.8 % (Fig. 3 A) and RQY to be 23.0 % (Fig. 3B), respectively. Fig. 3C describes the fluorescence decay curves of SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs. The fluorescence decay of SiNPs matched well with quadratic exponential decay model (χ2 = 1.026), and the fitting values were 3.27 ns (8.37 %) and 14.20 ns (91.63 %), of which the short lifetime value (less than 5 ns) might be caused by surface defects or surface trap rapid fluorescence radiation [44,45]. The fluorescence decay of Trf-SiNPs was in conformity with the first order exponential decay model (χ2 = 1.101) with the long lifetime of 14.59 ns and without short lifetime (less than 5 ns), which indicated the little surface defects or surface trap for the functionalization of transferrin. Meanwhile, fluorescence intensity of Trf-SiNPs still kept 92 % of the initial after 180 min irradiation, which demonstrated the strong optical stability of the as-synthesized Trf-SiNPs. The normalized fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged with the aqueous solutions pH of 2–11 range (Fig. 4 A) and the PBS buffer solutions pH of 4.5–9 (Fig. 4B) at 385 nm excitation wavelength and 800 nm excitation wavelength, which manifested the good resistance of Trf-SiNPs to the varied pHs.

The cell viability of HeLa was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Trf-SiNPs by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 5 , the cellular viabilities were more than approximately 95 % after being treated with the Trf-SiNPs concentrations of 0 ∼ 200 μg/mL for 24 and 48 h, which suggested the low cytotoxicity of Trf-SiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeted imaging of Trf-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The targeted imaging ability of Trf-SiNPs was investigated in HeLa cells and HepG2 cells. As displayed in Fig. 6 A, compared with the control groups, there was no significant change in HeLa cells morphology after being incubated with 100 μg/mL SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs for 8 h, which further demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs. Meanwhile, HeLa cells exposed to SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs both exhibited strong green dotted fluorescent signals at 405 nm excitation. Furthermore, the fluorescence signals of HeLa cells cultured with Trf-SiNPs were significantly brighter than those cultured with SiNPs, which indicated that more Trf-SiNPs entered into the HeLa cells in comparison with SiNPs due to the interaction between transferrin of Trf-SiNPs and transferrin receptor on the cells surface, revealing the targeted imaging ability of Trf-SiNPs in vitro. To further investigate the targeted effect of the Trf-SiNPs, HeLa cells and HepG2 cells were selected as the probe cells for the overexpression and the low-expression of transferrin receptors, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6B, HeLa cells exposed to 50 μg/mL Trf-SiNPs emitted green dotted fluorescent signals with the excitation of 405 nm. However, only few green dotted fluorescent signals were detected in HepG2 cells, which was caused by the less transferrin receptors of HepG2 cells compared with HeLa cells. The above results confirmed the targeted imaging effect of Trf-SiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeted two-photon imaging of Trf-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the two-photon-excited targeted imaging ability of Trf-SiNPs in vitro. Compared with the control groups, the HeLa cells cultured with 100 μg/mL SiNPs and Trf-SiNPs also exhibited the green fluorescence, but the fluorescence intensities of the cells cultured with SiNPs were weaker than those with Trf-SiNPs, which indicated the two-photon imaging and the targeted imaging ability of Trf-SiNPs (Fig. 7A). Moreover, the fluorescence signals of HepG2 cells cultured with Trf-SiNPs were weaker than those cultured with HeLa cells (Fig. 7B), which further manifested the targeted two-photon imaging ability of Trf-SiNPs in vitro. Combined with the results of MTT assay, the targeted single-photon imaging and the targeted two-photon imaging, Trf-SiNPs are available for targeted bioimaging in vitro.

In summary, we developed a facile and feasible targeted imaging probe of Trf-SiNPs, which were synthesized by SiNPs coupled with transferrin in the presence of EDC and NHS. Trf-SiNPs emitted blue fluorescence with the quantum yield of 28.8 %, featured strong stability, long fluorescence lifetime of 14.58 ns, good up-conversation property and low cytotoxicity. Results of fluorescence imaging demonstrated the good targeted single-photon imaging and two-photon imaging ability of Trf-SiNPs in tumor cells.

Hong-Li Ye: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xi-Wen He: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wen-You Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yu-Kui Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 22074069 and 21775077).
